glossary.def: A rule that maps a sample to a guess about a population parameter (e.g. the sample mean for the population mean). Being a function of random data, it has its own distribution, summarized by its bias and variance.
The standard deviation of an estimator's sampling distribution. For a mean it is sigma/sqrt(n), so error shrinks only as 1/sqrt(n) — halving it requires quadrupling the data.

An interval built so that, over many repetitions of the experiment, a stated fraction (e.g. 95%) of such intervals contain the true parameter. The guarantee is about the procedure's long-run coverage, not about any single interval.

The probability, computed assuming the null hypothesis is true, of a test statistic at least as extreme as the one observed. It is not the probability that the null is true, nor that the result is a fluke.

The probability (1 minus the Type II error rate) of correctly rejecting a false null — i.e. detecting a real effect. It rises with effect size, sample size, and a more lenient significance level alpha.

Analysis of Variance: an omnibus test that splits total variation into between-group and within-group sums of squares and compares them via the F-ratio F = MS_between / MS_within, testing whether three or more group means are equal.

An estimation method that picks the parameter value making the observed data most probable, by maximizing the log-likelihood. MLEs are consistent, asymptotically normal, and asymptotically efficient, underpinning regression and most ML loss functions.

Running many tests inflates the chance of at least one false positive (family-wise error). Corrections such as Bonferroni (test at alpha/m) or false-discovery-rate control restore error control; ignoring the problem is the core of p-hacking.

Ioannidis, J. P. A. (2005). Why Most Published Research Findings Are False. PLoS Medicine 2(8).
Open Science Collaboration (2015). Estimating the Reproducibility of Psychological Science. Science 349(6251).
Benjamini, Y. & Hochberg, Y. (1995). Controlling the False Discovery Rate. J. R. Stat. Soc. B 57(1).
Wasserstein, R. L. & Lazar, N. A. (2016). The ASA Statement on p-Values. The American Statistician 70(2).
Welch, B. L. (1947). The Generalization of Student's Problem When Several Different Population Variances Are Involved. Biometrika 34.
